hypothesis: Combining RSI oversold/overbought signals with a rising OBV trend ensures momentum and volume agree, while requiring ATR above its 14‑period average guarantees sufficient volatility for meaningful price moves; this reduces false entries in low‑activity periods and improves risk‑adjusted performance.

Combining an oscillator (RSI) with a volume‑based trend (OBV) and a volatility filter (ATR) reduces noise in a highly volatile crypto pair. A trade is only taken when RSI signals a clear momentum reversal, OBV confirms that the underlying volume is supporting the move, and ATR confirms that the market is sufficiently volatile to sustain the trade, thereby limiting false entries and improving risk‑adjusted returns.

Combining RSI momentum, OBV volume trend, and ATR volatility ensures that only strong, high‑volume, and sufficiently volatile moves trigger entries; exiting when more than half of these signals reverse reduces the risk of holding in a changing market.

Combining RSI momentum, OBV volume confirmation, and ATR volatility filtering ensures that trades are taken only when price, volume, and market noise all align, reducing false entries in choppy conditions and allowing a disciplined exit when the majority of these signals reverse.
Combining a trend filter (EMA) with momentum (RSI) and volume flow (OBV) reduces whipsaw entries in a volatile asset; ATR-based risk limits protect against large moves.
